entry.title: Re: SuccessFactors Learning Story Report Scheduled Class
entry.content:
Hi Sharat,

There would be no standard field in SAC LMS for this however you can make this work with the use of Calculated Columns in SAC and then using the SUM function in the front end of the report. I will show what I did as an example with screenshows showing how I made this. So in the below screenshot I made a calculated column with an IF function for the field Registration Status in the Enrolment Seat table where each time Registration Status = ENROLL (This was the registration status ID for Active Enrolment so in your case go to Learning Administration > Registration Statuses > Find the Registration Status ID of Enrolled and use this ID Value) it would display 1 otherwise display 0 if its not equal to it.

With this Calculated Column then you can select this column in the front end of your report where it will display a value of 1 or 0 in each row depending if the registration status = enrolled and then at the end of this column all you need to do is add a simple SUM function of this column adding all the 1s together which gives you the total number of users with a registration status of enrolled in this case. This would be one example of how you could do this with calculated columns

entry.title: Re: IBM MQ JMS Adapter for SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Hello @naveeneedara244 

Using SAP Cloud Connector installed in the OnPrem private network, and using connectivity service in SAP BTP, it's possible to use IBM MQ adapter using JMS type.

You can create a connection (location ID) using Cloud Connector which acts as reverse proxy. Then the SAP Integration Suite, integration runtime will connect via this tunnel to send the respective data through JMS adapter to IBM MQ. 

In the interface design editor, in the IBM MQ adapter, "Connection" tab, you have the "Location ID" which can be used to establish the connection between SAP Integration Suite (Cloud) to IBM MQ OnPremise.

Logically the "Proxy Type" in the same "Connection" tab, will be "OnPremise". 

Hope this helps.

Tiago Ribeiro

entry.title: Re: Issue with Feedback Feature in Backoffice (SAP Commer...
entry.content:
Dear @srinivasulu_mula ,

Based on the provided documentation, the feedback feature in SAP Commerce Cloud Backoffice is primarily designed for cloud environments and has specific limitations when used in local or on-premise systems.

Cloud Environment Requirement
The feedback feature that sends information directly to SAP through the Qualtrics survey system works optimally in cloud-hosted environments. When you encounter a "content blocked" message after clicking the feedback icon, this typically indicates:

Content Security Policy (CSP) restrictions are blocking the connection to external feedback collection endpoints
The feature may not be fully functional in on-premise or local development environments

Configuration Requirements for Local Systems:
To enable the feedback feature in a local system, you need to configure specific Content Security Policy (CSP) headers. The configuration must include the necessary domains for feedback collection:

backoffice.response.header.Content-Security-Policy=default-src 'self' https://*.qualtrics.com;
script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' https://*.qualtrics.com blob:;
connect-src 'self' *.qualtrics.com https://license.hybris.com/collect;
img-src * data:;
style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline';
font-src 'self' https://sdk.openui5.org/

The connect-src directive must include both *.qualtrics.com and https://license.hybris.com/collect for the feedback mechanism to work properly.

Limitations in Local Systems :
Even with proper CSP configuration, the feedback feature in local systems may have limitations compared to cloud environments because:

The feature relies on external Qualtrics services for survey delivery and data collection
Network connectivity and firewall rules in local environments may block these external connections
The feedback collection infrastructure is optimized for SAP's cloud hosting environment

Alternative Options
If the feedback feature is not working as expected in your local system, you can:

Disable the feature entirely by removing the Survey Launcher Widget through the Backoffice Orchestrator (press F4) or by modifying the widgets.xml file.
https://me.sap.com/notes/3474849 :Disable Qualtrics Feedback Survey popup in backoffice


Regards,
Prasanth
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: 如何在SLT系统里面Debuger以诊断表复制的错误
entry.content:
Dear customer

 

This is Chester again

The debugging can be started by entering "/h" in the transaction bar before you hit the execution button.

When a table is replicated, the SLT system actually execute the functions in Expert Functions from Creating Logging Table to Start Range Calculation 

Based on the different error, we could know at which function it fails, so we can reproduce this error by running this function in Expert Function. Before you execute this function, enter "/h" in the transaction bar, then execute, the system will jump into the debugging session.

This is more of a How-to question, rather than an actual error, which is out of the scope of Technical Support. I'm not able to help you further at this point.

If you face another error in SLT in future, maybe I can show you how to do it in details

 

Best regards,

Chester

entry.title: Re: How to remove a condition and create again?
entry.content:
Below is the recommended solution

1) Delete and Save:
Delete the condition in a new transport and release it.

2) Recreate with Correct Config:
Create the condition again with the required configuration changes in a second transport.

3) Transport Sequence:
Move both transports to PRD in the correct order (first deletion, then creation).

4) Check Dependencies:
Ensure the transport for the recreated condition also includes the decision table and data access definition.

5) Activate Decision Table:
After import, go into BRF+ or the relevant decision table app in PRD and explicitly activate the table.

entry.title: Re: Not able to assign Mitigation Control to the Monitor...
entry.content:
Hi @SapnaKarthikeyan,

 

Try this approach to achieve the requested setup:

  • Create user groups for both control monitors and owners via SUGR on SAP Access Control side (you can use the same group for both).
  • Assign the relevant users to these groups.
  • Maintain the required configuration on SAP Access Control to utilize these groups.
  • Map the specific users to your existing / previously maintained mitigation controls.

This should work as requested.

 

Regards,

Lukasz

entry.title: Re: Document Migration: Understanding User Difference Between Onboarding 1.0 and BizX
entry.content:
Dear @GabiMoraru,

Based on my experience, this situation is common in test or preview systems where onboardees may not be fully hired, or there are discrepancies due to different refreshes of the Onboarding and BizX systems.

This can occur especially if there is a configuration setting that prevents a full synchronization of the onboarding user when the candidate is hired via Manage Pending Hires.

Once you complete the onboarding process and proceed to hire through Manage Pending Hires, the system attempts to sync the user based on their userID (that happens via Manage data OnboardingCandidateInfo sync) . If, by the end of this process, the user in Employee Central has a different userID, the User Sync job will send a list of Employee Central users to Onboarding. The differing userID will then cause the system to recognize them as two separate users in Onboarding.

If this issue happens after fully complete a hire, that would require a ticket for root cause analysis.

In response to your question, there currently isn't a sync process that will merge these users now. The User Sync function only creates a new user if the userID is different and update the existing one based on index "userID".

Best regards,
Jacson
SAP Support

entry.title: Integrating Joule with Code-based Agents: Three Paths Forward
entry.content:
SAP Joule started as a conversational AI assistant is now a capable orchestrator that can delegate work to specialised agents — including agents you build yourself with your own code, frameworks, and models. Getting those two worlds to talk to each other is the subject of this post.

The Challenges and Options of integrating Joule with Code-based Agent

Felix Bartler's blog post, Joule A2A: Connect Code Based Agents into Joule, is the essential starting point for my exploring this space. It demonstrates  clearly and practically that you can wire a custom code-based agent into Joule using the open Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol and a pro-code Joule capability built with Joule Studio Code Editor. If you haven't read it, do that first.

That approach works well for fast agents. But it surfaces three constraints as following.

The Constraints

1. The 60-second clock

Joule's synchronous communication with a code-based agent (Bring Your Own Agent, or BYOA) carries a hard 60-second timeout. Reference to official Joule Studio documentation: "With synchronous communication, Joule expects the response from agent server in < 60 seconds.".  Deep research, complex reasoning chains, or agents that call slow external APIs routinely exceed this budget. When they do, the user gets an error and no response in Joule.

2. Agent Builder has no native A2A support as of March 2026

Direct A2A integration is only available to agents built with Joule Studio Code Editor (pro-code). Agents built with Joule Studio Agent Builder (low-code / no-code) have no equivalent A2A binding as of early 2026. Worth to mention that A2A support is planned in the roadmap of Joule Studio Agent Builder. Those who want to keep their Joule agent in Agent Builder, and connect it to an external code-based agent, will need a different path.

3. Lack of Real-time status update from remote agent for end user

End user doesn't receive any status update or message in realtime in Joule from remote agent about what it is going on, but have to wait until it finishes.

Three Paths Forward

These constraints open up three viable integration patterns, each with its own trade-offs:

 

 Option 1: Direct A2A integrationOption 2: Async RESTful APIOption 3: A2A Intermediate Client Proxy
Integration pathJoule (Code Editor) → A2A → Code-based AgentJoule (Agent Builder) → SAP Build Process Automation → Async REST → Code-based AgentJoule (Agent Builder or Code Editor) → A2A Client Service → A2A → Code-based Agent
ProtocolA2A (synchronous)REST (async, poll-based)A2A via SSE Streaming
Joule ToolingJoule Studio Code EditorJoule Studio Agent BuilderBoth
 Timeout~60 seconds~5 minutesNo hard blocking limit
Streaming ❌❌✅ Real-time
ComplexityLowMediumMedium
Best forFast agents (< 60 s)Long-running, (1~5 mins)Best UX; broadest Joule compatibility

Note: These three options merely summarise my exploration of Joule Integration with Code-based Agent. There are many other alternatives of A2A for agent  in a broader context , such as ACP (Agent Communication Platform), ANP (Agent Network Protocol) etc. which are not in the scope of this blog post. 

The rest of this post walks through each option in detail, using a concrete example throughout: the Deep Research Agent.

The Use Case: Deep Research Agent

The Deep Research Agent is an autonomous multi-agent system that, given a research topic, independently plans a research strategy, spins up multiple parallel sub-agents for simultaneous research including web search, reflecting on the gathered evidence, and synthesising a comprehensive research report with inline citations.

This sample code-based agent in this blog post is a fork of the deep research agent example of  langchain's deepagents SDK. It has been adapted to SAP Generative AI Hub via the SAP Cloud SDK for AI, and wrapped as a A2A server using A2A SDK and an Async RESTful API Server for integration with Joule.  The agent is deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry in run-time.

An overview demo

Agentic Diagram of Deep Research Agent

At a high level, the system is a two-tier agent hierarchy:

 

AI AgentDescriptionRoleUsed by
Deep Research AgentPlanning research, Orchestrating the sub research agent for multi-step research, Synthesizing the research reportRoot AgentEntry Point
Research AgentResearch and collect information for a give research topic with web searchSub AgentDeep Research Agent

Agentic Diagram of Deep Research Agent

Why is this a good test case? Because it is genuinely hard. A single research request may spawn several parallel sub-agents, each running multiple search-and-reflect cycles, before the orchestrator writes the final report. That easily exceeds 60 seconds — so it stresses all three integration patterns in a realistic way.

The Three Paths forward in Detail

Next, we'll dive into the details of each option, including high-level architecture, process flow, when-to-use and its source code if you would like to do some hands-on exercise.

Option 1: Joule Integration with Code-Based Agent via A2A

Sample code: deep_research_a2a

This is the most direct approach — and the closest to Felix's original blog post. A custom Joule capability (built with Joule Studio Code Editor) talks to the Deep Research Agent using the A2A protocol over HTTP. Joule acts as the A2A client; the Deep Research Agent acts as the A2A server.

joule a2a integration with deep research

How It Works

┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│        Joule as A2A Client          │  deep_research_agent_capability
│  Custom Pro-Code Joule Capability   │
└───────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                │  HTTP  (A2A protocol)
                ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│        A2A Starlette Server         │  app.py
│  AgentCard + AgentSkill metadata    │
└───────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                │
                ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│     DeepResearchAgentExecutor        │  agent_executor.py
│  Manages A2A task lifecycle          │
│  (working → artifact → complete)     │
└───────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                │
                ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│         DeepResearchAgent            │  agent.py
│  create_deep_agent via deepagents SDK│
│  and SAP Cloud SDK for AI            │
│                                      │
│  Orchestrator ──delegates─► Sub-agent│
│  (plan, synthesise, report) (search) │
└──────────────────────────────────────┘

The Deep Research Agent exposes a standard A2A AgentCard at /.well-known/agent.json, describing its capabilities and the deep_research skill. The Joule capability references this agent through a named SAP BTP Destination (deep-research-agent-a2a), keeping credentials out of code.

When a user asks a research question in Joule:

  1. Joule routes the request to the Deep Research Agent via A2A.
  2. The agent plans the research, delegates parallel tasks to research sub-agents, and waits for results.
  3. The orchestrator synthesises all findings and returns the final Markdown report as an A2A artifact (research_report) back to Joule.

The Timeout Constraint in Practice

The 60-second window is tight for deep research. The key lever is tuning two environment variables in the deployment manifest:

MAX_RESEARCHER_ITERATIONS: 2      # Limits research depth per sub-agent
MAX_CONCURRENT_RESEARCH_UNITS: 3  # Controls parallelism

Reducing these parameters speeds up completion at the cost of research quality. For topics where a 60-second budget is achievable, this option is the simplest to set up — one remote a2a-compatible and code-based agent deployed on Cloud Foundry, one BTP destination, and a Joule capability using an Agent Request Action.

Known Limitation

  • It has a hard timeout as 60 seconds. Any request longer than 60 seconds will result in an error of connection close by peer in remote agent.
  • Streaming is not supported by Joule's Agent Request Action. The user sees nothing until the full response arrives. Near the 60-second boundary, that can feel like a long, anxious wait.

When to Choose This Option

  • Your code-based agent typically completes within 60 seconds
  • You are working with Joule Studio Code Editor (pro-code capability)
  • You want minimal infrastructure — no intermediate services

Option 2: Joule Integration with Long-Running Code-Based Agent via Async REST API

Sample code: deep_research_api

When your agent consistently runs longer than 60 seconds, you need a fundamentally different communication model. This option replaces synchronous A2A with an asynchronous REST API and uses SAP Build Process Automation to manage the polling loop. We use this option in our sample.

One important caveat: The asynchronous callback model (bi-directional push notification) is supported Joule Agent Code Editor as Asynchronous API Request. So, a joule capability with Joule Studio Code Editor can wait up to 5 minute from remote agent until the result is returned. However, currently it requires the remote agent to have the same SAP Cloud Identity Service for authentication as Joule. Non-IAS authentication with remote agent must rely on the polling approach described above.

Architecture

Joule (Agent Builder Skill)
        │
        ▼
SAP Build Process Automation (Process)
  POST /research/jobs    ← submit job, receive job_id
  GET  /research/jobs/{id}  ← poll every ~60 s until completed
        │
        ▼
Deep Research FastAPI Service  (app.py)
        │
        ▼
DeepResearchAgent (LangGraph + deepagents + SAP Gen AI Hub)

Here, the Deep Research Agent is exposed as a RESTful service with three endpoints:

 

MethodPathDescription
POST/researchSynchronous — runs research and returns the completed report. Blocks until done (typically several minutes).
POST/research/jobsAsynchronous — submits a research job, returns a job_id immediately (HTTP 202).
GET/research/jobs/{job_id}Poll job status (running / completed / failed) and retrieve the result.

 

How It Works

  1. A Joule Skill (with Joule Studio Agent Builder) collects the user's research question.
  2. The skill triggers a SAP Build Process Automation process.
    Research Skill
  3. The process calls Submit Research Job (POST /research/jobs), receiving a job_id immediately.
  4. A looping condition polls Poll Research Job Result (GET /research/jobs/{id}) approximately every minute until status == "completed".
  5. The process extracts the result field (the Markdown report) and returns it to Joule as the skill output.
    Long-running Agent Process
  6. The process can be monitoring through Control Tower of SAP Build Process Automation
    Process Monitoring

Everything is orchestrated through a BTP Destination (deep-research-api) and an Action Project in SAP Build Process Automation. No custom code is required in the Joule layer.

Setting It Up

In SAP Build Process Automation, three components work together:

  • Action Project — defines REST actions (Submit Job, Poll Job) pointing at the deep-research-api destination.
  • Process — orchestrates submit → poll loop with a conditional repeat until status != "running".
  • Skill — wraps the process and surfaces it to Joule Agent Builder.

Pre-built artifacts are included in the sample to accelerate setup:

  • Long-Run Agent Process.mtar
  • Deep Research Skill.mtar

Import them directly from SAP Build Process Automation Lobby.

Effective Timeout

The practical ceiling is around 5 minutes — the outer boundary of SAP Build Process Automation's execution context. That's enough for complex multi-step deep research under reasonable configuration.

Known Limitation

The user experience is "fire and wait" — Joule shows a thinking indicator while the process polls in the background. There is no incremental output, which can feel opaque for lengthy research tasks.

When to Choose This Option

  • Your agent takes 1–5 minutes to complete
  • You prefer a low-code / no-code Joule experience (Agent Builder)
  • You are comfortable with SAP Build Process Automation for orchestration
  • Real-time streaming to the user is not a requirement

Option 3: Joule Integration with Code-Based Agent via A2A Client Service with Streaming

Sample code: a2a_client_service
Demo Recording: https://youtu.be/Gx7GM8ZxnM4

This stateless RESTful A2A Client Service(Proxy) enables non-A2A-compatible AI Agents connects to any remote A2A(Agent-to-Agent) AI Agent on the fly through RESTful APIs. It routes messages between non-A2A and A2A AI Agents as illustrated below.

Non-A2A AI Agent <=REST=> A2A Client Service (Proxy) <=A2A=> A2A-compliant AI Agent

Particularly, it allows Joule Agents(regardless built with Joule Studio Agent Builder or Joule Studio Code Editor) to integrate and communicate with remote A2A AI agents in streaming mode with enterprise-grade security. The target A2A Agent server URL and authentication credentials are resolved at request time from a named SAP BTP Destination Service entry, so no credentials are ever hard-coded or revealed in the service.

Why an Intermediary?

Before this option existed, three gaps made seamless integration frustrating:

  1. Joule's Agent Request Action (Code Editor) speaks A2A but has no streaming support.
  2. Joule's Streamed Message Action (Code Editor) supports streaming but not compliant with A2A.
  3. Joule Studio Agent Builder has no A2A support as of March 2026.  

The A2A Client Service bridges all three gaps in a single deployed service.

Architecture

Architecture of a2a-client-service

 

How it works

Joule Agent (Agent Builder or Code Editor)
  Streamed Message Action or REST Action
        │
        ▼
a2a-client-service  (Fastify / TypeScript on CF)
  POST /a2a/send              — synchronous proxy
  POST /a2a/stream            — raw A2A SSE stream
  POST /a2a/stream_normalised — normalised SSE stream
        │
        ▼
SAP BTP Destination Service
  (resolves auth: Basic / OAuth2ClientCredentials / OAuth2JWTBearer)
        │
        ▼
Remote A2A Agent  (e.g. Deep Research Agent)

 

The Streaming Endpoint: /a2a/stream_normalised

The key endpoint for Joule integration is POST /a2a/stream_normalised. It:

  1. Forwards the request to the remote A2A agent.
  2. Receives the raw A2A Server-Sent Events (SSE) stream (task, status-update, artifact-update).
  3. Normalises each event into a consistent JSON structure consumable by Joule's Streamed Message Action:
{
  "message": {
    "contextId": "...",
    "taskId": "...",
    "kind": "status-update",
    "name": "status-update",
    "parts": [{ "kind": "text", "text": "Researching AI agent frameworks...\n" }]
  }
}

Joule's Streamed Message Action extracts the text chunk using a JSONPath expression ($.message.parts[0].text) and renders it incrementally in the chat window — giving users a live view of the agent's progress as it happens.

What is the User Experiences like?

With streaming, the interaction feels natural and alive: Status updates arrive as each sub-agent reports progress. The final artifact (the research report) streams in as it is generated — no waiting for a silent spinner to resolve.

Joule Capability: Streamed Message Action

For Joule Studio Code Editor, the capability uses a streamed-message action pointing at the a2a-client-service:

- type: streamed-message
  method: POST
  system_alias: A2A_CLIENT_SERVICE
  path: /a2a/stream_normalised
  headers:
    Accept: "text/event-stream"
    Connection: "keep-alive"
    Cache-Control: "no-cache"
    Content-Type: "application/json"
  body: >
    {
      "message": "<? question ?>",
      "destinationName": "deep-research-agent-a2a"
    }
  response_chunk_ref: $.message.parts[0].text

For Joule Studio Agent Builder, the synchronous /a2a/send endpoint of the same a2a-client-service is exposed through an Action Project — giving Agent Builder users access to any A2A agent through a familiar REST action.

Security

Credentials never leave the BTP boundary. The destinationName in the request body is looked up at runtime by the SAP BTP Destination Service. The A2A Client Service inherit the supports all common auth types in destination:

Destination Auth TypeMechanism
BasicAuthenticationBase64 user:password header
OAuth2ClientCredentialsClient-credentials token fetch
OAuth2JWTBearerJWT exchange for outbound token
......

Known Limitation

When using /a2a/send synchronously through Agent Builder, the standard Action quota limits still apply (connection timeout: 1 min, socket timeout: 3 min). For truly long-running agents in Agent Builder, combine this with Option#2's async approach. For the best user experience with any duration, use the streaming endpoint via Code Editor — the SSE connection stays open without hitting the synchronous timeout.

When to Choose This Option

  • You want real-time streaming of agent progress to the user
  • Your agent may run longer than 60 seconds
  • You need to support both Agent Builder and Code Editor users against the same backend agent
  • You want to connect multiple different A2A agents by simply changing the destinationName — no capability changes needed

 

Choosing Your Path

The three options are not competing alternatives — they are solutions for different situations. Here is a simple decision guide:

Start with Option 1 if your code-based agent is fast (consistently under 60 seconds), you are already using Joule Studio Code Editor, and you want the most direct, least-infrastructure path. Felix's blog post and the deep_research_a2a sample are your starting point. Tune your code-based aent to keep execution within the time budget.

Choose Option 2 if your agent is genuinely long-running (up to 5 minutes) and you prefer a low-code Joule experience with Joule Agent Builder and SAP Build Process Automation handling the polling orchestration. The pre-built .mtar artifacts make setup fast. Just be mindful of the IAS requirement if you need the full async callback model.

Choose Option 3 if user experience matters most. Streaming gives users live feedback instead of a blank wait — which is significant when research takes several minutes. It also provides the greatest flexibility: one deployed proxy service connects Joule (regardless of Agent Builder or Code Editor) to any A2A-compliant agent, with credentials managed securely through BTP destinations. Swapping to a different agent is as simple as changing a destination name.

Wrapping up

What these three patterns represent, taken together, is the maturation of Joule as an orchestration layer. Joule does not need to be the capable agent for every task — it needs to connect the user to the right capable agent at the right time. A2A, async REST, and streaming proxies are three different connectors for doing exactly that.

The Deep Research Agent example used throughout this post is intentional. It is a realistic, non-trivial workload — it takes time, involves multiple agents working in parallel, and produces a rich artifact. Watching the same underlying agent behave differently under each integration pattern makes the trade-offs tangible rather than theoretical.

The full sample code, including all three integration patterns and the Deep Research Agent, is available in the btp-agentic-ai-use-cases GitHub repository. Each sub-folder is independently deployable with step-by-step instructions. Try the one that fits your context — and build from there.

entry.title: Re: SAP Business Technology Platform API Management layer.
entry.content:
APIM puts everything over the internet. If the organization has control over both the consumers and the service providers, APIM may add an unnecessary hop at times. It totally depends on your level of trust risk that you are okay to take. The APIM at the current level provides very minimal ways with organizing the APIs. For internal communications where you do not need to put explicit policies hitting them directly the OData layer on S4 may absolutely be fine. But for external consumption, APIM is a must to protect the organization from threats and attacks. 

Let's see what the other experts have to say.

entry.title: Production Quantity Control – Input vs Output Not Balanced in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

I am facing an issue related to production quantity control in SAP S/4HANA Cloud (Public Edition).

From a business perspective, during the production process, the total input quantity (raw materials consumed) should be equal to or greater than the total output quantity (finished goods + semi-finished goods + by-products).

Example:

  • Raw material consumed: 10,000 KG
  • Expected total output: ≤ 10,000 KG (distributed between finished goods, semi-finished goods, and by-products)

However, the system currently allows posting production where the total output quantity exceeds the consumed input quantity, without any error or blocking message.

Issue:
There is no system control or validation preventing:

  • Overproduction compared to consumed raw materials
  • Inconsistency between input and output quantities

Questions:

  1. Is there any standard configuration in SAP S/4HANA Cloud to enforce this control?
  2. Can the system be configured to block or warn when output quantity exceeds input quantity?
  3. Is this behavior standard, or does it require a custom enhancement (e.g., BAdI or validation logic)?
  4. Are there any recommended best practices to ensure quantity consistency in production orders?

Any guidance or similar experiences would be highly appreciated.

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Re: Employee's email address are not updated in the emplo...
entry.content:
Hello @vk36 

Thank you for your question.

Based on what you described, the key point is that, when you maintain an employee email through an HCM method such as Manage Workforce or by replicating workforce, the email is stored on the Employee Workplace Address of th Business Partner. That workplace address is not visible, and you cannot edit it in the usual BP apps (e.g., Manage Business Partner Master Data or Maintain Business Partner). So, if you updated the email in Manage Workforce and do not see the same email in the BP communication area, the reflects how the system is designed, not necessarily a replication failure. KBA 2924397 mentions that, when you create email and address details through Manage Workforce or SuccessFactors integration, they are saved to the employee workplace address of the BP, and that workplace address is not shown in th BP apps.

So, the direct answer to your question is:

  1. Regarding the email not being updated in BP, this is because the email maintained from Manage Workforce is stored in the employee workplace address layer of the Business Partner, and that layer is not displayed din the standard BP apps as mentioned above.
  2. Regarding how you can resolve it, in most cases, there is nothing for you to correct in your configuration. The correct validation is for you to check the employee related apps and factsheet instead of expecting the email to appear in the standard BP communications tab. If you suspect a real synchronisation issue, then you should also check the follow up synchronisation logs in Monitor Data Synchronisation and, where relevant, Message Monitor Overview.

See the following reference for more insights.

2905918 - FAQs: Replicate Basic Master Data for Workforce from External HR Systems - SAP for Me

So, my suggested next steps should be:

  • Recheck the employee email in Manage Workforce to confirm the source update was saved.

  • Use Employee Factsheet or Maintain Business Users to validate the email in the correct standard place.

  • If the email is not visible there, check Message Monitor Overview for follow up errors in workforce to BP synchronization.

  • If your landscape uses more than one workforce integration channel, verify that the employee is not being updated through multiple channels. The reason is, the system does not support updating the same workforce data through parallel channels after replication is active. See KBA 2813743 for more details.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Event-driven architecture
entry.content:
Thanks — that makes sense for simple consumption patterns. My challenge is that we have multiple downstream consumers with different processing needs, and I’m not sure iFlows alone can handle routing, filtering, retries, and lightweight enrichment without becoming tightly coupled.

I’m still trying to understand the boundary between:

  • Event Mesh → iFlow (simple, single-purpose)

  • Event Mesh → CAP (when logic, orchestration, or multi-consumer coordination is needed)

If you’ve implemented multi-consumer patterns at scale, how did you avoid duplication and keep the architecture maintainable?

entry.title: Re: SAP Business Technology Platform API Management layer.
entry.content:
Thanks for the perspective — that helps. Where I’m still unsure is how to balance governance vs performance when we have mixed traffic patterns (internal apps, partner apps, and external consumers).

From what I’ve seen:

  • Direct S/4 OData calls work for low‑latency internal workloads, but we lose central policies, quota control, API versioning, and lifecycle governance.

  • API Management gives us consistent security (OAuth2SAMLBearerAssertion, JWT validation, IP filtering), analytics, and traffic management — but adds an extra hop.

  • Private Link + APIM seems like a middle ground, but I’m not sure how widely it’s used in real projects.

So I’m still trying to understand the real‑world pattern:

Do most customers enforce APIM for all traffic (internal + external) to maintain a single governance layer, or do they selectively bypass it for high‑throughput internal scenarios?

Would love to hear how others have handled this at scale without creating two parallel integration standards.

entry.title: Preparing for the Interview
entry.content:
As we’ve seen in our last blog, candidate fraud can be a serious issue in the hiring process, compromising the integrity of your recruitment efforts. Now that you know how some SAP Fieldglass features can help you in the candidate selection process, in this blog, we’ll dive into features that can help you during the interview process. 

Hiring the right candidate can significantly impact the success and dynamics of your project. However, resumes and applications don't always tell the whole story. Before you even contact a candidate, there are several proactive techniques you can employ to recognize red flags: 

🚩Running initial checks on applicants, such as background screenings and reference confirmations 

🚩Using video chat tools to verify the identity of candidates before setting up interviews 

🚩Training the hiring team on typical scam methods and warning signs 

🚩Using safe communication methods to talk with candidates 

🚩Avoiding personal e-mail addresses or unprotected messaging apps 

Background Check Information 

With the Background Check Integration feature, you can add a background check to the SAP Fieldglass hiring process. It's a process in which info is sent and received between SAP Fieldglass and a selected background check provider. This setup guarantees that a supplier can't accept a work order until the background check is complete and passed. Once this feature is on, all candidates can have a background check. Here's the general process: 

  1. The supplier starts the background check and is redirected to the provider's website. 
  1. The supplier fills in the required info for the job seeker, and the background check provider does the check. 
  1. Once done, the results are posted to the SAP Fieldglass app.  

You can choose to skip the background check for individual work orders. For more information about the setup, see Background Check Integration. 

Here’s an example of an optional setup for the Job Seeker module: 

  1.  Go to Admin Menu 
  2.  Select Messaging under Messaging 
  3.  Select Job Seeker under Module 
  4.  Select the Background Check you want under Action

Microsoft Office 365 Calendar Configuration  

The Microsoft Office 365 Calendar Integration is another feature that can help you before your interviews with job seekers. Integrating Microsoft Office 365 Calendar with SAP Fieldglass can help mitigate potential candidate fraud in several ways.  

Calendar invites instantly separate serious candidates from fake ones. Genuine applicants accept and confirm, while the others ghost. Real-time interviewer availability lets you schedule quickly, reducing the window for fraud candidates to slip through. Integrated calendars eliminate double-booking, so candidates get the attention they deserve while your team stays organized. Automated reminders keep everyone on track and flag potential no-shows. For remote teams, synchronized time zones eliminate confusion and miscommunication. Plus, every scheduled event creates an audit trail you can reference if suspicious activity arises. It's streamlined, secure, and very effective.  

To use this feature: 

  • Make sure that the integration between SAP Fieldglass and Office 365 Calendar is correctly set up. For this, see Microsoft Office Calendar Configuration to check the configuration steps. 
  • Regularly monitor and audit calendar activities, looking out for any unusual patterns or discrepancies. 

SAP Fieldglass and SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Integration 

Integrating SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central with SAP Fieldglass can also help to mitigate fraud risks in your selection process. This integration creates a powerful fraud detection system that protects your recruitment process. By connecting these platforms, you gain real-time access to comprehensive employee data that enables you to verify candidate identities against existing records and instantly flag inconsistencies or duplicate applications under different names. The system automatically validates compliance requirements, such as work permits and visa status, ensuring candidates are legally eligible before interviews begin. 

Beyond basic verification, the integration establishes centralized access controls that allow only authorized users to view or modify candidate information, while maintaining a complete audit trail of all recruitment activities. This transparency makes it easy to spot unusual patterns or suspicious behavior. Best of all, you can configure intelligent risk scoring models that automatically evaluate candidates based on multiple data points, automatically detecting high-risk profiles for deeper investigation. 

To make the most of these integrations, ensure that both systems are properly configured. See the SAP Fieldglass and SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Integration for more details.  

How about checking some SAP Fieldglass features that can help you during your candidate selection phase? You can use them to create accountability and reduce fraudulent applications. 

Next, we’ll see what measures you can take during the actual hiring process that can help you to increase security when hiring candidates. Stay tuned!

 

 

 

For the purposes of this guide, “candidate fraud” means deliberate misrepresentation of identity, credentials, work history, or qualifications during recruitment. This guide is preventive and educational; it does not make legal determinations.

entry.title: Re: Copy control for item category HUPB is missing in LF...
entry.content:
Dear @baba,

For the open deliveries SAP does document a supported cleanup approach to remove outbound deliveries from the billing due list the outbound delivery must be deleted or rejected

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3546631

If the delivery is EWM-relevant and deletion is no longer possible after distribution to Cloud WM, SAP documented workaround is to reverse goods issue, cancel open warehouse tasks / reverse picking, reduce the delivery quantity to 0 and post goods issue again so the delivery is completed on the LE side with zero quantity. SAP also notes that if billing already exists, it should be reversed first before the EWM-side reversal proces

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3052822

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3126573

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3719730

Summary:

The product gap itself must be handled through SAP feature request process because copy control cannot be extended by customers in Public Cloud. The current backlog can be reduced only through SAP-supported delivery deletion/rejection or for EWM-managed deliveries, through the documented reversal and zero-quantity closure process

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Delivery getting blocked for credit segment even if the credit segment not assigned to customer
entry.content:
Delivery is blocked at outbound delivery by credit check; credit check log shows messages such as "Credit check executed with rule ZC", "Credit limit 0.00 has been exceeded", "Oldest open item is less than 010 days overdue", and "Check was not successful".


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Delivery getting blocked for credit segment even if t...
entry.content:
Hi, please find below.

Cause:

The sales organization is assigned to a credit segment in customizing, so deliveries created for that sales organization are evaluated in that segment even if the customer is not extended to it; without maintained credit data, a default limit of 0.00 applies and the check fails.

Refer to: What happens during a credit check if no credit segment has been created?

 

Resolution:

  1. Review the customizing “Assign Sales Area to Credit Segment” and confirm that the sales organization-to-credit-segment mapping is intended.
  2. If the mapping is not intended for this process, adjust or remove the assignment so deliveries for that sales organization are not evaluated in the unintended credit segment.
  3. If the mapping is intended, extend the business partner to the assigned credit segment and maintain an appropriate credit limit (and related credit data) for that segment.
  4. Optionally review the credit profile/risk class settings if they are part of your automatic credit control design.
  5. Recreate or recheck the delivery and post goods issue; verify the credit check passes as per the maintained limits

Reference KBA : 3739105 - Delivery blocked at outbound delivery by credit check even when customer is not assigned to the credit segment - SAP for Me

entry.title: Billing Document created but no eDocument appears in Manage Electronic Documents / EDOC_COCKPIT
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I’m currently working on SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition and trying to validate the first step of an e-invoicing flow:

Goal: Create a customer billing document (Create Billing Documents app / “VF01-like” flow) and automatically get an eDocument instance created and visible in Manage Electronic Documents (F4306) and/or eDocument Cockpit (EDOC_COCKPIT).

Current situation / issue

  • Scope item 5XU (Document and Reporting Compliance) is activated in the system.
  • I created a billing document successfully.
  • However, when I check Manage Electronic Documents (F4306), I find no eDocument for this invoice (even with broad filters).
  • Same issue when checking via eDocument Cockpit (EDOC_COCKPIT): nothing appears for the billing document number.

So there is likely a missing configuration step somewhere, but I can’t identify it.

Configuration already done (CBC)

In CBC / “Configure Your Solution”, I have maintained and set to Completed the following (screenshots available):

Document and Reporting Compliance

  • ✅ Activate Document and Reporting Compliance Features

Electronic Documents

  • ✅ Activate Additional eDocument Instance Creation
  • ✅ Maintain General Parameters for eDocument
  • ✅ Assign eDocument Type to Accounting Document Type
  • ✅ Assign eDocument Type to Billing Document Type

Questions

  1. In Public Cloud, should an eDocument be created automatically at billing document posting by default (once the above is set)?
  2. What are the typical missing prerequisites when the billing document exists but no eDocument is created?
    • Output Management configuration needed (Output Parameter Determination / EXTOM)?
    • Missing mapping (eDocument process / type / country scenario)?
    • Additional activation required at company code / country level beyond the CBC steps above?
  3. Is there any recommended way to trace why the eDocument instance is not created (application log, monitoring app, specific Fiori app)?

Target architecture (end-to-end)

The project target flow is:

  1. Generate eDocuments in S/4 Public Cloud
  2. Send them to SAP DRC
  3. Transform to a file format (e.g., UBL)
  4. Send to GENERIX via SFTP
  5. GENERIX sends to the government platform

Right now I’m blocked at step 1 (eDocument instance is not created / not visible).

Any guidance, checklist, or pointers to the correct missing configuration would be greatly appreciated.
Thanks a lot!

Best regards,
Zakaria
SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Sales  SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

entry.title: Re: Creating a new Operating COA in Public Cloud that is...
entry.content:
Good day @Ebrahim_Mohamed 

Thank you for your question.

The simple answer is NO. Reason is, it is not the standard supported approach for you to create a brand-new operating chart of accounts to replay YCOA. However, the Public Cloud offers you the option to adapt YCOA to your business needs and, if you require, you can create a separate alternative or local chart of accounts in your own namespace for local reporting or statutory numbering needs.

Regarding you question on the impact on Account Determination:

  • The operational account determination logic still works against the operating chart of accounts, which is YCOA for the company code.
  • If you add new operating G/L accounts in YCOA, you must maintain the required entries in the relevant account determination SSCUIs. Otherwise, your postings can fail with errors. See KBA 3090084 for more details.
  • Creating an alternative or local COA does not replace YCOA based account determination. It mainly adds local account numbers and reporting structure on top of the operating YCOA setup.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/32ed3c6435ea46afb4dbab6839fabe8e.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a624a4a6d1d8473eb95fb42660127cfe/90fea76092254ab0b6cbe0019a55e285.html?locale=en-US

You may consider validating the following steps in your system:

  • Open Manage Operating Chart of Accounts and confirm the operating COA in use. If your requirement is for local numbering, use Edit Chart of Accounts List to create an alternative chart of accounts, not a replacement operating COA.

  • Open Automatic Account Determination and check all relevant entries for your YCOA accounts that you use in your posting.

  • Carry out a test posting and confirm whether you need to maintain any missing YCOA account determination entries.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP Cloud Integration (CI/CPI) - Identify & Manage Unused iFlows (integration flows)
entry.content:
Over time, SAP Cloud Integration (CI/CPI) tenants, especially larger ones tend to accumulate integration flows that are no longer needed. These may include old test flows, integration scenarios tied to decommissioned systems, or temporary interfaces created during projects and never removed.

Manually identifying such unused integration flows is difficult and time‑consuming. SAP Cloud Integration provides standard APIs that expose deployment status and recent execution history. By combining these APIs, we can build a simple, transparent, and repeatable solution to highlight unused integration flows and optionally clean them up.

Problem Statement

Integration teams often face questions such as:

  • Which integration flows are still being used?
  • Which flows have silently stopped executing?
  • Which deployed flows can safely be reviewed or removed?

Solution Overview

The solution described in this blog is implemented as a controller integration flow. It uses only standard SAP Cloud Integration APIs and consists of the following steps:

  1. Retrieve all currently deployed integration flows
  2. Retrieve message processing logs from the monitoring API
  3. Correlate deployment status with execution evidence
  4. Identify unused integration flows
  5. Generate easy‑readable reports (JSON and CSV)
  6. Optionally undeploy unused integration flows (explicitly controlled)

What Do We Mean by Unused Integration Flows?

Due to SAP CI monitoring retention limits, this solution intentionally uses a clear and conservative definition:

An unused integration flow is a deployed integration flow that has not shown any execution activity within the available monitoring window and is older than a configurable grace period.

Rather than making assumptions about long‑term execution history, the solution classifies unused flows based on what the platform can reliably confirm.

Distinction Between Inactive and No Message Processing Logs

When the inactivity threshold is configured to a value lower than the monitoring retention window (for example, 14 days), the solution can provide additional insight:

Inactive Integration Flows:

  • Execution logs exist
  • The flow executed previously but not within the last N days (eg: 14 days)

No Message Processing Logs:

  • No execution records exist within the monitoring window
  • This may indicate that the flow never executed recently or that any execution happened outside the retention period (30 days)

When the inactivity threshold is equal to or greater than the monitoring retention period, both cases are safely grouped under a single concept: unused integration flows.

High-Level Architecture

The controller integration flow is structured into modular steps:

  • Retrieve deployed integration flows
  • Retrieve message processing logs
  • Build an execution map
  • Detect unused integration flows
  • Generate reports
  • Optional undeploy branch

This modular design keeps the logic easy to understand, test, and extend.

Scripts Used in the Integration Flow

extractDeployedIflows

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def body = message.getBody(String)
    def json = new JsonSlurper().parseText(body)

    if (!json?.d?.results) {
        throw new RuntimeException("Invalid response from IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts API")
    }

    def deployedIflows = []

    json.d.results.each { r ->

        // Only Integration Flows
        if (r.Type == "INTEGRATION_FLOW") {

            // Consider active runtime states only
            if (r.Status in ["STARTED", "STARTING"]) {

                deployedIflows << [
                    id         : r.Id,
                    name       : r.Name,
                    version    : r.Version,
                    status     : r.Status,
                    deployedOn : r.DeployedOn,
                    deployedBy : r.DeployedBy
                ]
            }
        }
    }

    message.setProperty("DEPLOYED_IFLOWS", deployedIflows)
    message.setProperty("DEPLOYED_COUNT", deployedIflows.size())

    return message
}

This script parses the Integration Runtime Artifacts API response and collects all deployed integration flows along with metadata such as deployment timestamp.

buildExecutionMapFromLogs

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.util.XmlSlurper

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def xmlText = message.getBody(String)
    def xml = new XmlSlurper(false, false).parseText(xmlText)

    /*
      Map:
        IntegrationFlowName -> Latest LogStart
    */
    def executionMap = [:]

    xml.entry.each { entry ->

        // CPI-safe namespace access
        def props = entry.content."m:properties"

        if (!props) {
            return
        }

        def iflowName = props."d:IntegrationFlowName"?.text()
        def logStart  = props."d:LogStart"?.text()

        if (iflowName && logStart) {

            def existing = executionMap[iflowName]

            // Keep only the latest timestamp
            if (!existing || logStart > existing) {
                executionMap[iflowName] = logStart
            }
        }
    }

    message.setProperty("EXECUTION_MAP", executionMap)
    message.setProperty("EXECUTION_COUNT", executionMap.size())

    return message
}

This script parses the Message Processing Logs response and builds a map that links each integration flow ID to its most recent execution timestamp found in monitoring.

detectUnusedIflows

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.time.LocalDateTime
import java.time.temporal.ChronoUnit

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def deployed = message.getProperty("DEPLOYED_IFLOWS")
    def execMap  = message.getProperty("EXECUTION_MAP")
    def days     = message.getProperty("INACTIVITY_DAYS") as Integer

    if (!deployed || execMap == null || days == null) {
        throw new RuntimeException(
            "Missing required properties: " +
            "DEPLOYED_IFLOWS=${deployed != null}, " +
            "EXECUTION_MAP=${execMap != null}, " +
            "INACTIVITY_DAYS=${days != null}"
        )
    }

    def now = LocalDateTime.now()
    def inactiveIflows = []

    deployed.each { i ->

        def deployedOn = LocalDateTime.parse(i.deployedOn)

        // Grace period: skip newly deployed flows
        def deployedDaysAgo = ChronoUnit.DAYS.between(deployedOn, now)
        if (deployedDaysAgo < days) {
            return
        }

        def lastRun = execMap[i.id]

        if (lastRun) {
            // Execution exists in monitoring
            def last = LocalDateTime.parse(lastRun)
            def inactiveDays = ChronoUnit.DAYS.between(last, now)

            if (inactiveDays >= days) {
                inactiveIflows << [
                    id            : i.id,
                    name          : i.name,
                    deployedOn    : i.deployedOn,
                    lastExecution : lastRun,
                    inactiveDays  : inactiveDays,
                    reason        : "INACTIVE"
                ]
            }
        } 
        
        
        else {
            // No execution detected in monitoring
            inactiveIflows << [
                id            : i.id,
                name          : i.name,
                deployedOn    : i.deployedOn,
                lastExecution : null,
                inactiveDays  : deployedDaysAgo,
                reason        : "NO_MONITORING_LOGS"
            ]
        }
        
    }

    message.setProperty("UNUSED_IFLOWS", inactiveIflows)
    message.setProperty("UNUSED_COUNT", inactiveIflows.size())

    return message
}

This script compares deployed integration flows with available execution data. It applies a grace period and identifies which flows can be considered unused.

generateJsonAndCsvReport

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonOutput
import java.time.LocalDateTime

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def unused = message.getProperty("UNUSED_IFLOWS") ?: []
    def days   = message.getProperty("INACTIVITY_DAYS")

    // -------- JSON Report --------
    def report = [
        scanTimestamp   : LocalDateTime.now().toString(),
        inactivityDays  : days,
        unusedCount     : unused.size(),
        unusedIflows    : unused
    ]

    def reportJson = JsonOutput.prettyPrint(JsonOutput.toJson(report))

    // Set JSON as response body
    message.setBody(reportJson)
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")

    // -------- CSV Report --------
    def csvLines = []
    csvLines << "Id,Name,DeployedOn,LastExecution,InactiveDays,Reason"

    unused.each { u ->
        csvLines << [
            u.id,
            u.name ?: "",
            u.deployedOn ?: "",
            u.lastExecution ?: "",
            u.inactiveDays != null ? u.inactiveDays.toString() : "",
            u.reason ?: ""
        ].join(",")
    }

    def reportCsv = csvLines.join("\n")

    // -------- MPL Attachments --------
    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)
    if (messageLog != null) {

        // JSON attachment
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString(
            "Unused_Integration_Flow_Report.json",
            reportJson,
            "application/json"
        )

        // CSV attachment
        messageLog.addAttachmentAsString(
            "Unused_Integration_Flow_Report.csv",
            reportCsv,
            "text/csv"
        )
    }

    return message
}

This script produces a JSON and a CSV report containing all unused integration flows. Both reports are attached to the Message Processing Log for auditing and review.

Integration Flow Steps:

For Integration Content API Reference, please refer this link

1. Setting the inactivity days property to explicity specify if you want to add a grace period Eg: 10 days or 14 days apart from the standard 30 days retention. Setting a property to automatically undeploy the unused flows 

2. Call the API /api/v1/IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts to get all the deployed integration flows in the tenant

3. Use the script extractDeployedIflows

4. Call the API /api/v1/MessageProcessingLogs to get all the message logs in the tenant

5. Use the script buildExecutionMapFromLogs

6. Use the script detectUnusedIflows

7. Use the script generateJson&CsvReport

8. If the undeploy unused iflows needs to be automated then use a router as below

9. Since we need to provide iflow ids one by one to API, we need to use a General Splitter. General Splitter works well if the format is in xml. Hence, I have used a standard JSON to XML converter

10. In the General Splitter, I have set the XPATH expression as below

11. Set iflow id property as below

12. Call /api/v1/IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts('${property.id}') with DELETE operation

JSON Report:

CSV Report:

How to Use the Results

In Non‑Production Systems:

  • Clean up abandoned test integration flows
  • Reduce tenant clutter
  • Improve troubleshooting efficiency

In Production Systems:

  • Identify candidates for review
  • Support governance and lifecycle discussions
  • Optionally undeploy flows with explicit control

Limitations and Transparency

This solution respects SAP Cloud Integration monitoring retention limits. It does not attempt to infer execution history beyond what the platform provides. For long‑term historical analysis, execution timestamps would need to be stored externally.

Conclusion

By combining deployment information with recent execution evidence, this solution provides a practical and transparent way to manage unused integration flows in SAP Cloud Integration.

It delivers immediate value in non‑production systems and meaningful governance insights in production, all while relying solely on standard CPI capabilities.

I invite you to share your thoughts in the comments below. It helps others who are navigating similar journeys and contributes to a community of shared knowledge. I look forward to hearing from you!

entry.title: The Handshake at the Heart of the Data Product Economy: Data Contracts with SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
by Wolfgang Epting, SAP Business Data Cloud Blackbelt & Customer Advisor Chief Expert, SAP

In my previous blogs, I outlined how SAP Business Data Cloud operationalizes the four principles of Data Mesh, and how the Data Product Economy transforms data from a stored commodity into a dynamic currency — traded across domains for uptime, compliance, and trust.

But every economy depends on one foundational institution: the contract.

Without a contract, a market is not a market. It is a bazaar — informal, unreliable, and ultimately unscalable. The same logic applies to the Data Product Economy. You can build the most elegant Data Mesh architecture, establish domain ownership, and publish hundreds of data products — but if there is no formal agreement governing what is being shared, under what conditions, and with what guarantees, you have not built a data economy. You have built a sophisticated way to distribute uncertainty.

This is what Data Contracts solve. And this is precisely where SAP Business Data Cloud is making a decisive move — not just as a concept, but as an operational reality. 

What Is a Data Contract — and Why Do So Many Organizations Avoid Them?

A Data Contract is a formal, explicit agreement between a data producer and a data consumer. It defines the structure, semantics, quality standards, access conditions, and governance obligations that govern the exchange of a Data Product.

In theory, everyone agrees that Data Contracts are necessary. In practice, most organizations avoid them — because implementing them has historically required significant manual effort, bespoke tooling, and sustained cross-domain coordination that central IT simply cannot sustain at scale.

The result is a familiar pattern: Data Products are published without formal interfaces, consumers build fragile dependencies on undocumented schemas, and the first breaking change cascades into a cross-organizational incident. Trust erodes. Shadow copies proliferate. The Data Mesh devolves into a more sophisticated version of the data silos it was designed to replace.

The question, therefore, is not whether organizations need Data Contracts. It is whether they can implement them without creating more friction than they eliminate.

SAP Business Data Cloud answers this question with two complementary capabilities: the Data Product Studio and Usage Agreements.

The Data Product Studio: Contracts by Design, Not by Accident

The SAP BDC Data Product Studio is the central authoring environment for all data products within the platform — SAP-managed, custom-managed, and third-party. But its most significant contribution to the Data Contract model is architectural: it enforces a strict separation between design-time and run-time.

This means that before a single data pipeline is wired up, the producer commits to a formal definition: the schema, the semantic description, the domain assignment, and the version. The contract is established first. The implementation follows.

This sequence matters enormously. In most traditional data engineering workflows, the interface is an afterthought — documented retroactively, if at all. In the Data Product Studio, the interface is the product. Everything else is the delivery mechanism.

Semantic Versioning reinforces this principle. Every change to a data product definition is tracked, versioned, and communicated through a structured lifecycle. Consumers are not exposed to silent schema changes. Breaking changes trigger a new major version; backward-compatible extensions increment a minor version. The rules are predictable, the expectations are explicit, and the contract evolves in a way that both parties can anticipate and prepare for.

Consider a concrete example from the Finance domain:

The Finance domain publishes a "Cost Center Hierarchy" data product in the Data Product Studio. The definition specifies the output structure, the refresh frequency, the owning domain, and the semantic mapping to the SAP Knowledge Graph. When the organizational restructuring in Q3 requires a schema change, the Data Product Studio generates a new major version — and all downstream consumers in Controlling and FP&A are notified automatically. No silent breakage. No emergency fix. No trust deficit.

This is what a Data Contract looks like when it is operationalized at platform level.

Usage Agreements: The Demand Side of the Contract

 If the Data Product Studio addresses the supply side of the Data Contract — defining what is being offered and under what terms — then Usage Agreements address the demand side: formalizing who consumes the data, for what purpose, and under what conditions.

In the SAP BDC governance model, access to a data product is not granted by default. Every consumer who discovers a data product in the BDC Catalog must explicitly request access, articulate their use case and accepting the terms defined by the owning domain. This request triggers a Usage Agreement — a governed, documented, and auditable record of the access relationship.

The Usage Agreement workflow involves three key roles:

The Requestor discovers the data product in the catalog, evaluates the metadata and semantic description to confirm it fits their use case, and submits an access request with a documented use-case statement.

The Data Steward, acting on behalf of the Domain Owner, reviews the request, verifies alignment with the domain's data governance policies, and either approves or rejects it — with full documentation of the decision in both cases.

Additional roles — such as Legal, Works Council, or Budget Responsible — can be integrated into the approval workflow required by regulatory or organizational policy. This is not an edge case: in regulated industries and public sector organizations, multi-party approval is a compliance requirement, not an option. This extension is not included in the current planned scope, but remains a natural next step on our roadmap.

The result is a complete, machine-readable audit trail of every data access relationship within the platform. At any point, a Data Steward can answer the questions that regulators, auditors, and governance boards most commonly ask: Who has access to this data? When was access granted? What use case was it granted for? Has access been reviewed?

Back to our Finance example:

The FP&A team in Regional Finance Central Europe requests access to the "Cost Center Hierarchy" data product to build a headcount allocation model. Their request includes the use case statement and is routed to the Finance domain's Data Steward. After review, access is approved — and the Usage Agreement is recorded. When internal audit reviews data access controls in Q4, the complete access history is available without any manual reconstruction.

This is what federated governance looks like when it works.

The Data Contract in Practice: Closing the Loop

Taken together, the Data Product Studio and Usage Agreements close the Data Contract loop that most Data Mesh implementations leave open.

The producer commits to a formal interface through the Studio — versioned, semantically described, and domain-owned.

The consumer commits to a formal access relationship through the Usage Agreement — use-case specific, reviewed, and auditable.

The platform enforces the terms on both sides — through semantic versioning, governance workflows, and the BDC Catalog as the single point of discovery and exchange.

This model does not require a separate Data Contract toolchain bolted onto the architecture. It does not depend on manual documentation maintained in wikis or spreadsheets. It is embedded in the operational fabric of SAP Business Data Cloud — which means it scales with the organization, not against it.

And in the era of Agentic AI, this is no longer a governance nicety. It is a prerequisite. Joule Agents executing autonomous business processes cannot operate on undocumented data. They require a semantic layer they can trust, interfaces they can rely on, and access conditions that are explicitly defined. Without the Data Contract infrastructure that BDC provides, AI agents are not accelerators. They are liabilities.

Conclusion: Trust Is Not a Feature. It Is a Contract.

The Data Product Economy is not built on data. It is built on trust — and trust, in an organizational context, requires formal agreements.

Data Contracts are how you institutionalize trust between data producers and data consumers. They are how you prevent the Data Mesh from fragmenting into a new generation of data silos. And they are how you ensure that AI, analytics, and intelligent applications operate on a foundation that is not just technically sound, but organizationally accountable.

With the Data Product Studio and Usage Agreements, SAP Business Data Cloud makes Data Contracts operational — not as a future roadmap item, but as a platform capability available today.

Don't just build a Data Mesh. Don't just build a Data Product Economy.

Build the contracts that make it trustworthy.

Thanks to Florian Neukirch @FlorianN for his support. 

This blog is part of an ongoing series on Data Mesh and the Data Product Economy with SAP Business Data Cloud. Read the previous entries:

  • From Vision to Reality: Enabling Data Mesh with SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Beyond Data Mesh: Entering the Data Product Economy with SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: Period Determination Issue in “Close Period for Material Master Records” with Custom FYV
entry.content:
Hi SAP expert, 

I would like to seek clarification regarding the app “Close Period for Material Master Records/Allow posting to previous period” in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

From my understanding, this app displays the current period, previous period, and last period of the previous year. However, I would like to better understand which SSCUI configuration controls the determination of these periods.

As an example, consider a scenario where a fiscal year variant (e.g. SZ) is defined as year-dependent, with fiscal years maintained for multiple years. Assume the fiscal year is structured as:

  • From 21 March YYYY to 20 March YYYY+1

For instance, for FY2025:

  • Period 01/2025 > Last day: 20 April 2025
  • Period 02/2025 > Last day: 20 May 2025
  • …
  • Up to Period 12/2025 > Last day: 20 March 2026

Based on this setup, the system will show 002/2025 as the current period in the apps' Close Period for Material Master Records.

Therefore, I would like to kindly ask:

  • Which SSCUI configuration is responsible for controlling the period determination in this app?
  • Is it solely driven by the Fiscal Year Variant, or are there additional configurations (e.g. MM-related settings) that influence the current period?

Any guidance or insights would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Re: SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices
entry.content:
"SAP Datasphere provides a rich and flexible set of ETL capabilities"

I used many ETL tools before, and I personally find the opposite.

No programmability: if I need to read 100 CSV or Excel files with dynamic names => I need an external ETL tool.

If I need to retrieve a dynamic source => no way.

No batching: (it is not an ETL tool, it suffer of OOM issues when processing large dataset, there are some batching / partitioning / HEX_DISK features, but it is not built to do batching).

No ETL classical transformations (lookup, surrogate keys, SCD ... etcetera).
No programmability in orchestration (no loop, no conditionals, no variables, no structured output).

No reusable flows (ports like embedded dataflows in SAP Data Services).

And The sad truth is that they keep innovating with other features that are built on this fragile architecture:

Example: Replicaiton flows with HDLF target spaces: only if the source is from 3-4 source types and only if the source has primary key, and only if the target has primary key and CDC delta enabled.

They need to fix the base and give more programmability options to be considered a "rich and flexible set of ETL capabilities".

for now it is a good "Dimensional Modeling and semantic tool".

entry.title: Re: What I Wish I Knew
entry.content:
I wish I had known earlier how powerful the connected enterprise vision becomes when you look at SAP end-to-end—not as individual products, but as one integrated ecosystem driving the full value chain.

From Signavio for process understanding and optimization, to LeanIX for enterprise architecture visibility, to SAP Cloud ALM for lifecycle management, and SAP Central Business Configuration (CBC) for standardized setup, all the way through to SAP Core ERP—it creates the foundation for a truly harmonized enterprise.

Extending beyond the core, solutions like SAP Ariba for procurement, SAP Concur for travel and expense, SAP Fieldglass for external workforce, SAP SuccessFactors for HR transformation, and Replicon for time tracking further strengthen the enterprise backbone by connecting front, middle, and back-office processes.

With SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) and embedded AI capabilities like Joule, this ecosystem evolves even further—enabling real-time insights, intelligent automation, and AI-driven decision-making across the entire enterprise.

Early on, these often feel like separate platforms or programs. What I’ve learned over time is that the real value comes when they are connected—when process, people, data, and execution flow seamlessly across the enterprise.

That’s when SAP moves from being a system landscape to becoming a true intelligent, connected enterprise platform.

entry.title: Re: Production Quantity Control – Input vs Output Not Bal...
entry.content:
Dear @c_walid,

In Public Cloud the documented confirmation controls are focused on order-based checks such as underdelivery, overdelivery, QM results, future dates and goods movement error handling. SAP also states that during confirmation the goods receipt quantity corresponds to the yield quantity and if components are backflushed the goods issue quantity is derived from the confirmed quantities and the component quantity per manufactured item. That means the standard logic is driven by order quantity/tolerances and BOM-recipe relationships not by a separate delivered validation that consumed input must be greater than or equal to total output

So for your specific questions:

Question 1.
Based on the SAP-documented confirmation settings, no standard CBC/confirmation parameter is described for that exact mass-balance rule. The standard checks SAP lists are the ones above, and they do not include a delivered validation comparing cumulative raw-material consumption to cumulative finished/semi-finished/by-product output

Question 2.
Not in standard for that exact comparison. What standard can do is warn or block when confirmed yield + rework exceeds the orders overdelivery tolerance. SAP explicitly documents the Overdelivery key in confirmation parameters for that purpose

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-manufacturing-execution/configuring-confirmations-for-production-orders

Question 3.
For the exact business rule you described this should be treated as enhancement-required. SAP supported extensibility direction for both Confirm Production Order (CO15) and Confirm Production Order Operation (CO11N), including Custom Logic and the extensibility documentation points to Badi-based custom logic for these manufacturing apps. That is the supported Public Cloud direction if you want to validate and block the posting with your own rule

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/d99d67cc419f4d519f080e158a16ee73.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/eb381da44986470cadfa80891d81fe6d.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/dd313229871540368053d17eacf1e2ea.html

Question 4.
Without custom logic the best standard safeguards are:

  • keep overdelivery tolerance at 0 or very low
  • use backflush and automatic goods receipt where possible
  • minimize manual GI/GR postings outside the controlled confirmation flow

This does not create the exact input-versus-output control, but it does keep quantities aligned to the planned BOM/recipe-driven confirmation logic as closely as standard allows

Summary:

Your observed behavior is standard in Public Cloud. SAP standard can control overdelivery versus order quantity/tolerance but not a true input consumed vs total output produced validation for that exact rule a supported custom enhancement in Custom Logic/Badi is the right path

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud Advance Foreign Currency Valuation p...
entry.content:
Hello @dlwsupport 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your description, there are really two separate issues in your case:

  1. Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation is creating revaluation lines without trading partner. KBA 3452330 provides specific details for that situation.
  2. For the currency imbalance itself, the Post Currency Adjustments app is intended to adjust account balances in or more currencies, but I am not aware of the option to independently change Balance Transaction Currency while deliberately leaving all other related currency amounts untouched. Balance Transaction Currency is part of the journal entry logic, not a standalone operating currency setting like company code currency or group currency. That explains why USD can sometimes be corrected with Post Currency Adjustments, while Balance Transaction Currency is harder to isolate.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/f22f2aca75404b5f84becb9b246023b2.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/282a17e9885f4dc980a461ca682ed7d1.html?locale=en-US

I would not advice that you try to “manually force” only the Balance Transaction Currency column to zero independently if the imbalance is caused by the original postings or by valuation postings without the correct trading partner. The safer approach is for you to:

  • First correct the trading partner derivation/account assignment rule for the valuation posting
  • Then analyse which original open items or prior correction posting are leaving a non-zero balance
  • Then use Post Currency Adjustments only where the app supports the currency correction you need.

  • If the remaining imbalance is specifically in Balance Transaction Currency, then you should consider correcting the source journal entries or post a controlled manual correction entry that keeps the required dimensions, especially trading partner, rather than trying to edit only one display column in isolation.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris
