entry.title: Enhancement Ideas on SAP Build Work Zone - Permission, Workspace Admin, Content Approval
entry.content:
Hello Work Zone users and administrators,

As our company also started using SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition as an "intranet" as a start, there are few opportunities of enhancement that, we think, will greatly help large scale organisations with complex matrix and content management to adopt the product more effectively.

We have raised few enhancement requests and wish you take few mins to vote and support our ideas to SAP.

(1) Select which Sub-workspace the User be Made Workspace Admin WHEN Making User Main Workspace Admin
Improvement Request Details - Customer Influence
(#371329)
Currently, when a user is made Workspace Admin for the a main workspace, the user is also automatically made Workspace Admin in ALL the sub-workspaces that are nested under that main workspace.

We proposed to enhance to prompt to allow the admin to select which sub-workspace(s) should the user be made workspace admin WHEN making him/her Workspace Admin for the main workspace.

The proposed enhancement will address the following problem:

(1) For companies that will use sub-workspaces as departmental/sectional site, this enhancement will prevent a user (who is a representative of a department) to have unnecessary/excessive admin right for other sub-workspace(s).

(2) With this enhancement, the access to a private folder created in a departmental/sectional sub-workspace will only truly be accessible to the relevant Workspace Admin(s), instead of all the workspace admin that inherits the right from the main workspace, bring more meanings to create a private folder.

(3) This enhancement will allow the departmental user to maintain the right to mirror content from their responsible sub-workspace (folders) to the Main Workspace (folders) which serves as the HOME of a company site to display content that comes from the department, at the same time ensuring a stricter admin permission on sub-workspace level.

The proposed enhancement will allow smoother dynamics of content sharing and folder management, and adopt flexible workspace administrator matrix to cope with more complex organisation structure that naturally needs to invite more content owner to come into play.

(2) Mirror Multiple Content to Multiple Destination Folder at a Time
Improvement Request Details - Customer Influence
(#371324)

Currently, workspace admin can only mirror content/file to a destination folder one by one.

We propose to enhance the platform to allow mirroring multiple content/file to multiple destination folders within/across workspaces/sub-workspaces at a time.

The proposed enhancement will great reduce the time spent in mirroring a certain volume of content/files to multiple workspaces, drastically reduce the clicks and navigation in the content/files mirroring process.

This would be greatly beneficial to large scale companies/conglomerates with multiple workspaces for their subsidiaries/business units who have central content owner who is responsible for distributing global content/files to those sub-workspaces. Currently, it is very painful for the global content owners to mirror content one by one with repetitive clicks and navigation.

(3) Set Content Approval Not only for Workspace with Full Collaboration Level
Improvement Request Details - Customer Influence
(#360103)

Currently, only when a workspace collaboration level is set to 'Full', can content approval be configured for that workspace.  In cases where a workspace is set to 'Read Only' and only workspace admin can publish content (e.g. blog post), it will be beneficial to allow content approval on workspace admin level to facilitate and ensure content governance before the content gets published to the general members.    This will lessen the content drafting, editing and reviewing off the platform and offline back-and-forth communication.

In cases where a workspace is set to 'Read Only' and only workspace admin can publish content (e.g. blog post), it will be beneficial to allow content approval on workspace admin level to facilitate and ensure content governance before the content gets published to the general members.

We strongly believe companies that use WorkZone as one-way communication and that has strong and strict content publishing guideline would be beneficial from this suggestion. This suggestion will add a layer to ensure content aligns the company's tone and style before the content gets published within the WorkZone application.

Thank you for reading through and your vote!

Cheers,
Max

entry.title: Configuration for Automatic HU Creation During GR in SAP EWM Cloud 2602
entry.content:
Dear SAP Team,

I am currently checking the configuration for automatic Handling Unit (HU) creation during the inbound process. While creating an inbound delivery, the system should automatically create HU numbers for each product based on the defined packaging size. However, in SAP Extended Warehouse Management Cloud version DIGITS, I am unable to find the relevant configuration.

Could you please provide the configuration steps and the application name required for this scenario?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Configuration for Automatic HU Creation During GR in...
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

Thank you for your question regarding the automatic creation of Handling Units (HUs) during the inbound process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Embedded EWM).

In this system, automatic HU creation is achieved through automatic packing functionality. This behavior is not controlled directly by a single setting in the inbound delivery, but rather by packing rules and master data, such as packing instructions and/or packaging specifications, depending on the scope and configuration.

To enable this functionality, you should first ensure that packing rules are properly defined. This can be done using the relevant apps available in the Warehouse Management scope, where you define how products are grouped into Handling Units, including quantities per HU and the corresponding packaging materials.

In addition, the system must be able to determine the correct packing rule. This is typically controlled through condition-based determination, where criteria such as product and warehouse are used so the system can select the appropriate packing logic during processing.

Once the configuration is in place, the system can automatically create Handling Units based on the maintained packing quantities. For example, if a product is defined with a packing size of 25 KG per HU and the inbound quantity is 50 KG, the system can create 2 HUs accordingly. Similarly, a product with a packing size of 30 KG would result in 1 HU for a quantity of 30 KG.

For more information and the exactly steps to follow, you can refer to the SAP Help Portal documentation for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

Automatic Packing – Packing with Packing Instructions | SAP Help Portal

Automatic Packing in the Inbound Delivery | SAP Help Portal

Thank you very much! If you have any further questions, feel free to follow up in the thread.

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Import History from EC not include employee
entry.content:
Hi @Maria851 , thank you for your screenshots but unfortunately I can't see what the issue could be as everything seems correct vs the dates in your example.

Here is what I would do if I had access to this environment.

1. Duplicate your template into a test one that you can then inactive using the instructions at the bottom of this blog.

2. In the new template go to Plan Details > Define Planners > and check "Include Inactive Employees"

3. Create an eligibility rule that points only to the 2 UserIDS (JobInfo.UserId = ABC123 [OR] JobInfo.UserId=ABC456) for these two users and map it to the template (Plan Setup> EC settings as per screenshot you sent me). Then Save and go to Plan Setup > Import Employee History from Employee Central. 

  • Check whether both records are being retrieved this time : If so then something in our old rule is excluding the one you want to see (it can be data or it can be a conflict of dates, impossible for me to say without access to EC).
  • If you still see only one, then it means the missing one has an issue in EC and as per David's suggestion I would check in Provisioning

Thanks 

Xavier

entry.title: BiWhy – Audit DB vs CMS DB
entry.content:
Disclaimer: BiWhy is available again for FREE to everyone on biwhy.net

A typical SAP BusinessObjects landscape is built with great care: 2–3 tiers, redundancy, load balancers, disaster recovery – and then, quite often, both the CMS DB and the Audit DB end up on the same database host.

This is a mistake.

The two databases have very different personalities.

The CMS DB is the heart of the system.
But it is not the most DBA-friendly database in the world. Some data is encrypted, and part of the structure is handled more by the application than by the database itself.

Because of that, normal SQL analysis is limited, administration is less convenient, and there can be some performance overhead.

On the positive side, it is very small.

The Audit DB, on the other hand, uses a standard star schema. It is fairly SQL-friendly if you query by keys, but producing human-readable output usually requires many joins.

For standard reporting, the usual choice is the Audit Universe. For troubleshooting, though, before BiWhy I usually preferred a SQL console because it performed much better. Now, of course, I use BiWhy for everything.

For this discussion, two things matter most:

  • Growth – it can grow quickly, depending on system usage and audited events.
  • Load – queries against it can be large and resource-intensive.

So one database is tiny but essential. The other can become large and demanding, but is auxiliary.

Intuition says they should be isolated.

The “Lazy” Architecture Trap

Why do we put them together?

Usually, it is just a lazy line of thought: “Well, I’m already setting up a database server, so I might as well put both schemas there.”

And it is pretty obvious why that logic fails: even in separate schemas, they are still fighting for the same CPU, memory, and disk I/O.

Why Isolation Is Your Best Friend

A common pushback to isolation is: “Our DBAs are pros. They will manage the isolation.”

But if something can go wrong, it will go wrong.

Multiple times, we saw systems go down or suffer severe performance impact because of exhausted log space, table space, disk space, or CPU caused by the Audit DB.

Even customers who must keep Audit enabled would usually prefer temporary Audit downtime over the whole system going down. And customers who had the databases separated had either no system issue at all, or a much smaller one, when Audit DB problems appeared.

Plus, separate databases are much easier to troubleshoot.

Resource Misallocation: The Silent Performance Killer

Resource-wise, both databases are relatively lightweight and do not require massive hosts. The main exception is disk space for the Audit DB – that is simply proportional to your audit level, retention period, and system activity.

The load profile:

  • Audit DB – one constant INSERT stream and a few episodic, but potentially very expensive reads, unless a third-party tool is constantly polling it
  • CMS DB – primarily read-heavy traffic

The CMS / Audit DB layer is no exception to the general BOBJ sizing pattern – it is often either heavily oversized or clearly undersized.

But the most painful case, by far, is when the CMS DB does not have enough CPU.

In one case, the host running the CMS and Audit DBs had less than 5% of the total CPU capacity of the whole system. That database host was constantly redlining, while the other 95% of CPU capacity sat mostly idle on the application nodes.

My top list of BOBJ resource misallocation:

  • CMS DB – underpowered CPU
  • Tomcat / web tier, and to a lesser extent the CMS tier – oversized CPU and RAM
  • Processing tier – undersized RAM

Funny fact: there were a few escalations on a fairly popular RDBMS where the Audit DB was running out of disk space mostly by storing… NULLs. The database allocated 16 KB for a TEXT-like field even when the value was NULL, and the Audit DB was sending plenty of NULLs.

CMS DB Health

For CMS DB health, start with Avg Query Time.

Do not pay much attention to Avg Commit Time – remember, the CMS DB is mostly read-intensive.

Ignore Longest Query Time – it is always crazy high.

Practical thresholds:

  • 8–20 ms – best
  • around 50 ms – good
  • around 300 ms – likely a sign of a system problem

When you are already in trouble, an even better indicator is the number of used connections.

People often try to fix the problem by increasing the default 14 to higher numbers. In most cases, that only makes things worse.

Normally, BOBJ does not need many DB connections. If the number starts rising, it is usually because responses are getting slow. By adding more connections, you add more load on the DB, more contention, and more locks – so the situation gets worse.

By the way, outdated statistics, especially after massive updates, are a common reason for poor CMS DB performance.

Audit Health

A sign of an Auditing problem is 100% audit thread utilization for a long time. By itself, this is not yet a failure, but it is a reason to start paying attention.

One peculiarity: the CMS shows the last value, not the current one, for thread utilization. So even if auditing has already stopped, or that CMS is no longer the auditor, the value may still remain at 100%.

In any system, only one CMS acts as the auditor, so the connection to the Audit DB should be 1 on that CMS and 0 on the others. Normally, you should see one 1 and the rest 0s.

If you see 1s everywhere, that is a problem. It can happen if CMS processes crashed, if DB connections were lost… or simply because CMSs were restarted manually, which can produce the same picture. 😊

Rapid accumulation of audit events in queue means the audit subsystem is not keeping up – or that there is a more serious problem.

Moderate accumulation during peak hours can be normal, as long as it is cleared later.

A sharp drop usually means the problem was resolved – for example, the DB connection was restored, a lock disappeared, and so on.

entry.title: Agentic AI for SAP Data Migration: From Assessment to Sign-Off (Series 2, Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 4 OF 6

Agentic AI for SAP Data Migration: From Assessment to Sign-Off

Five migration agents built with LangGraph and SAP AI Core — landscape complexity analysis, data quality validation, runbook generation, execution monitoring, and post-migration reconciliation — covering ECC to S/4HANA, HANA Cloud migration, and general DMLT workstreams.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 4 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: SAP data migration projects are among the most complex, high-risk workloads in enterprise IT — and also among the most repetitive. The same assessment questions, the same validation checks, the same runbook structure, the same reconciliation patterns appear in every project. Agentic AI is uniquely suited to this workload because it can plan, execute multi-step tasks, and reason over SAP-specific context autonomously. This article builds five migration agents using LangGraph and SAP AI Core that cover the full migration lifecycle — from initial landscape assessment through post-migration sign-off.

Series Context

This is Part 4 of the Agentic AI in Practice series. Parts 1–3 established the agentic AI architecture, ReAct agent pattern, and multi-agent orchestration on BTP. This part applies those patterns to a domain where agentic AI delivers immediate, measurable value: SAP data migration.

Data migration is the backbone of every SAP transformation — ECC to S/4HANA, on-premise HANA to HANA Cloud, legacy system consolidations. The workstreams are well-defined, the failure modes are known, and the documentation burden is enormous. All three characteristics make migration an ideal first production deployment for SAP AI agents.

WHY DATA MIGRATION IS THE RIGHT STARTING POINT FOR SAP AGENTS 

The Migration Agent Opportunity

Every SAP data migration project runs through the same lifecycle regardless of source system, target system, or migration tool. Assessment, validation, documentation, execution, and reconciliation. The steps are known. The questions are known. The failure patterns are known.

What makes migration painful is not the complexity of individual steps — it's the volume, the manual coordination between steps, and the time pressure that compresses quality at every stage. A consultant who has run ten migrations knows exactly what to check. An agent can apply that same knowledge at the speed and scale that human consultants cannot.

Migration Phase

Current Approach

Typical Time

Agent Advantage

Landscape assessment

Manual review of Z-objects, custom tables, interfaces

2–3 weeks

Agent analyzes in hours with consistent coverage

Data quality validation

Rule-based checks, manual exception review

Ongoing throughout project

Agent applies rules continuously, flags exceptions with context

Runbook generation

Template-based, manually populated per object

1–2 weeks

Agent generates object-specific runbooks from landscape analysis

Execution monitoring

Manual log review, escalation judgment calls

During migration window

Agent monitors in real-time, classifies and escalates autonomously

Reconciliation & sign-off

Manual count comparisons, exception investigation

Post-migration

Agent cross-validates counts, generates sign-off report with exceptions

ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW 

Agent Architecture for SAP Migration Workstreams

The five migration agents in this article share a common architecture pattern. Each agent is implemented as a LangGraph StateGraph — a stateful directed graph where nodes represent reasoning or action steps and edges represent conditional routing based on agent decisions.

SAP Source System → BTP + Cloud Connector → LangGraph Agent → AI Core (GPT-4o) → Output / Action

LangGraph is chosen over simple chain patterns because migration workflows are not linear. An assessment agent may discover a Z-table that requires deeper investigation, loop back, gather more data, and then continue. A monitoring agent may encounter an exception that requires human escalation before proceeding. LangGraph's conditional edges and state management handle these loops and branching decisions cleanly.

The SAP AI Core connection uses the generative-ai-hub-sdk from Series 1. GPT-4o is the recommended model for migration workstreams — its SAP code and schema knowledge consistently outperforms other models for ABAP analysis, table structure interpretation, and technical documentation generation.

All agents in this article connect to SAP systems via BTP Destinations and Cloud Connector — no direct database connections, no ABAP changes required. Read-only access is sufficient for assessment, validation, monitoring, and reconciliation agents. The runbook generation agent requires no SAP connectivity at all — it works from the output of the assessment agent.

AGENT 1 OF 5 

  ▶  Landscape Complexity Assessment Agent 

Agent 1: SAP Landscape Complexity Analyzer

The assessment agent analyzes a source SAP landscape — ECC, S/4HANA, or legacy systems — and produces a structured complexity report covering custom object density, interface dependencies, data volumes, and migration risk indicators. This replaces the manual 2–3 week scoping phase with an agent-driven analysis that completes in hours.

Connect to SAP → Inventory Z-objects → Analyze dependencies → Assess data volumes → Generate risk report
# Agent 1: SAP Landscape Complexity Assessment
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
from typing import TypedDict, List, Dict
import requests, json
 
# Define agent state — carries context between nodes
class AssessmentState(TypedDict):
    sap_system:      str    # 'ECC' | 'S4HANA' | 'HANA_CLOUD'
    resource_group:  str
    z_objects:       List[Dict]
    interfaces:      List[Dict]
    data_volumes:    Dict
    risk_indicators: List[str]
    complexity_score: str   # 'LOW' | 'MEDIUM' | 'HIGH' | 'CRITICAL'
    report:          str
    messages:        List
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
# Node 1: Inventory custom Z-objects via RFC/OData
def inventory_z_objects(state: AssessmentState) -> AssessmentState:
    """
    Query TADIR for custom Z/Y objects — tables, programs, function modules.
    Uses BTP Destination → Cloud Connector → RFC_READ_TABLE or OData.
    """
    # Query via BTP Destination (configured with Cloud Connector for on-prem)
    response = requests.get(
        f"{os.environ['SAP_ODATA_URL']}/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZMT_LANDSCAPE_SRV/ZObjectSet",
        headers={'Authorization': f"Bearer {get_sap_token()}",
                 'Accept': 'application/json'}
    )
    z_objects = response.json().get('d', {}).get('results', [])
    state['z_objects'] = z_objects
    return state
 
# Node 2: AI analyzes Z-objects for migration complexity
def analyze_complexity(state: AssessmentState) -> AssessmentState:
    z_summary = json.dumps(state['z_objects'][:50])  # Top 50 for context
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP migration architect.
        Analyze these custom Z-objects and assess migration complexity.
        Consider: object types, naming patterns, volume, interdependencies.
        Return JSON: {complexity_score, risk_indicators[], key_findings[]}'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Analyze Z-objects for {state["sap_system"]}:\n{z_summary}')
    ]
    response = llm(messages)
    analysis = json.loads(response.content)
    state['complexity_score'] = analysis['complexity_score']
    state['risk_indicators'] = analysis['risk_indicators']
    return state
 
# Node 3: Generate structured assessment report
def generate_report(state: AssessmentState) -> AssessmentState:
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''Generate a professional SAP migration
        complexity assessment report. Include executive summary,
        complexity score with justification, top risk items,
        and recommended migration approach.
        Format as structured markdown.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'''
System: {state['sap_system']}
Z-Objects: {len(state['z_objects'])} custom objects
Complexity: {state['complexity_score']}
Risks: {state['risk_indicators']}''')
    ]
    state['report'] = llm(messages).content
    return state
 
# Build LangGraph
assessment_graph = StateGraph(AssessmentState)
assessment_graph.add_node('inventory', inventory_z_objects)
assessment_graph.add_node('analyze', analyze_complexity)
assessment_graph.add_node('report', generate_report)
assessment_graph.add_edge('inventory', 'analyze')
assessment_graph.add_edge('analyze', 'report')
assessment_graph.add_edge('report', END)
assessment_graph.set_entry_point('inventory')
assessment_agent = assessment_graph.compile()

AGENT 2 OF 5 

  ▶  Data Quality and Mapping Validation Agent 

Agent 2: Data Quality and Mapping Rules Validator

The validation agent applies migration mapping rules to source data records and identifies quality issues — missing mandatory fields, invalid values, orphaned references, duplicate keys — before migration execution. It uses LangGraph's conditional edges to loop back on ambiguous records rather than failing fast.

Load mapping rules → Sample source data → Apply rules → Ambiguous? → AI resolves → Quality report
# Agent 2: Data Quality and Mapping Validation
class ValidationState(TypedDict):
    object_type:     str    # e.g. 'CUSTOMER', 'MATERIAL', 'VENDOR'
    mapping_rules:   List[Dict]
    source_records:  List[Dict]
    failed_records:  List[Dict]
    ambiguous_records: List[Dict]
    validation_pass: bool
    quality_report:  str
 
# Node: Apply mapping rules — flag failed and ambiguous records
def apply_mapping_rules(state: ValidationState) -> ValidationState:
    failed, ambiguous = [], []
    for record in state['source_records']:
        issues = []
        for rule in state['mapping_rules']:
            field = rule['source_field']
            if rule['mandatory'] and not record.get(field):
                issues.append(f"Missing mandatory field: {field}")
            if rule.get('valid_values') and record.get(field) not in rule['valid_values']:
                if rule.get('allow_ai_resolution'):
                    ambiguous.append({'record': record, 'issue': issues})
                else:
                    failed.append({'record': record, 'issues': issues})
    state['failed_records'] = failed
    state['ambiguous_records'] = ambiguous
    return state
 
# Conditional edge: route ambiguous records to AI resolution
def route_validation(state: ValidationState) -> str:
    if state['ambiguous_records']:
        return 'resolve_ambiguous'
    return 'generate_quality_report'
 
# Node: AI resolves ambiguous mapping decisions
def resolve_ambiguous(state: ValidationState) -> ValidationState:
    for item in state['ambiguous_records']:
        messages = [
            SystemMessage(content=f'''You are an SAP {state['object_type']}
            master data expert. Resolve this mapping ambiguity.
            Return JSON: {{resolution, confidence, target_value}}'''),
            HumanMessage(content=f'Record: {json.dumps(item)}')
        ]
        resolution = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
        if resolution['confidence'] == 'LOW':
            state['failed_records'].append({**item, 'resolution': resolution})
    state['ambiguous_records'] = []
    return state

The conditional edge pattern is the key architectural advantage of LangGraph for migration validation. Simple chain patterns fail fast on ambiguous records. The LangGraph approach routes them to AI resolution first — only escalating to failed status when confidence is genuinely low. This significantly reduces false failure rates in migration validation runs.

AGENT 3 OF 5 

  ▶  Migration Runbook Generator Agent 

Agent 3: Migration Runbook and Documentation Generator

The runbook agent takes the assessment report from Agent 1 and generates object-specific migration runbooks — step-by-step execution guides, rollback procedures, and sign-off checklists — tailored to the specific objects, volumes, and risk profile of the landscape being migrated. This replaces the most time-consuming documentation phase of any migration project.

Assessment report → Identify migration objects → Generate per-object runbook → Generate rollback plan → Compile full runbook
# Agent 3: Migration Runbook Generator
class RunbookState(TypedDict):
    assessment_report: str
    migration_type:    str  # 'ECC_TO_S4' | 'HANA_CLOUD' | 'DMLT'
    migration_objects: List[str]
    object_runbooks:   Dict[str, str]
    rollback_plan:     str
    full_runbook:      str
 
def extract_migration_objects(state: RunbookState) -> RunbookState:
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''Extract the list of SAP objects
        requiring migration runbooks from this assessment report.
        Return JSON array of object names only.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=state['assessment_report'])
    ]
    state['migration_objects'] = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
    return state
 
def generate_object_runbooks(state: RunbookState) -> RunbookState:
    runbooks = {}
    for obj in state['migration_objects']:
        messages = [
            SystemMessage(content=f'''Generate a detailed SAP migration
            runbook for this object in a {state['migration_type']} scenario.
            Include: pre-checks, extraction steps, transformation rules,
            load sequence, validation steps, rollback trigger criteria.
            Format as numbered steps with time estimates.'''),
            HumanMessage(content=f'Object: {obj}\nContext: {state["assessment_report"][:500]}')
        ]
        runbooks[obj] = llm(messages).content
    state['object_runbooks'] = runbooks
    return state
 
def generate_rollback_plan(state: RunbookState) -> RunbookState:
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''Generate a comprehensive rollback plan
        for this SAP migration. Include: rollback triggers, sequence,
        data restoration steps, system validation after rollback,
        and communication plan. Be specific to the migration type.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Migration: {state["migration_type"]}\nObjects: {state["migration_objects"]}')
    ]
    state['rollback_plan'] = llm(messages).content
    return state

Runbook quality is directly proportional to assessment report quality. The runbook agent generates object-specific procedures from the assessment context — vague assessment outputs produce generic runbooks. Run Agent 1 thoroughly before triggering Agent 3 for production migration documentation.

AGENT 4 OF 5 

  ▶  Migration Execution Monitor Agent 

Agent 4: Real-Time Migration Execution Monitor

The monitoring agent watches migration execution logs in real time, classifies exceptions by severity and type, determines whether exceptions require immediate human escalation or can continue, and generates a running status report for the migration team. It uses LangGraph's loop pattern to continuously process new log entries throughout the migration window.

Poll migration logs → Parse new entries → Classify exceptions → Escalate? → Continue or halt → Update status
# Agent 4: Migration Execution Monitor
class MonitorState(TypedDict):
    log_source:         str   # log file path or API endpoint
    last_processed_line: int
    exceptions:         List[Dict]
    critical_flags:     List[str]
    escalation_required: bool
    migration_status:   str   # 'RUNNING' | 'WARNING' | 'CRITICAL' | 'COMPLETE'
    status_report:      str
 
def poll_migration_logs(state: MonitorState) -> MonitorState:
    """Read new log entries since last processed position."""
    with open(state['log_source'], 'r') as f:
        lines = f.readlines()
    new_lines = lines[state['last_processed_line']:]
    state['last_processed_line'] = len(lines)
    # Parse new entries — store as raw text for AI classification
    state['_new_log_entries'] = '\n'.join(new_lines[-100:])  # Last 100 lines
    return state
 
def classify_exceptions(state: MonitorState) -> MonitorState:
    if not state.get('_new_log_entries'):
        return state
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP migration expert.
        Classify exceptions from these migration logs.
        Return JSON: {exceptions: [{type, severity, message, action}],
        overall_status: RUNNING|WARNING|CRITICAL,
        escalation_required: bool,
        escalation_reason: str}'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Migration logs:\n{state["_new_log_entries"]}')
    ]
    result = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
    state['exceptions'].extend(result['exceptions'])
    state['migration_status'] = result['overall_status']
    state['escalation_required'] = result['escalation_required']
    return state
 
# Conditional edge: escalate critical issues, loop for non-critical
def route_monitoring(state: MonitorState) -> str:
    if state['migration_status'] == 'CRITICAL':
        return 'escalate'
    if state['migration_status'] == 'COMPLETE':
        return 'final_report'
    return 'poll_migration_logs'  # Continue monitoring loop

AGENT 5 OF 5 

  ▶  Post-Migration Reconciliation and Sign-Off Agent 

Agent 5: Post-Migration Reconciliation and Sign-Off

The reconciliation agent performs post-migration validation — comparing source and target record counts, validating key field mappings, identifying orphaned records, and generating a sign-off report with exception summary. This is the final gate before a migration is declared complete.

Source counts → Target counts → Cross-validate → Investigate gaps → Generate sign-off report
# Agent 5: Post-Migration Reconciliation
class ReconciliationState(TypedDict):
    migration_objects:  List[str]
    source_counts:      Dict[str, int]
    target_counts:      Dict[str, int]
    discrepancies:      List[Dict]
    investigated_gaps:  List[Dict]
    sign_off_status:    str   # 'APPROVED' | 'CONDITIONAL' | 'REJECTED'
    sign_off_report:    str
 
def fetch_source_counts(state: ReconciliationState) -> ReconciliationState:
    counts = {}
    for obj in state['migration_objects']:
        # Query source system record counts via BTP Destination
        response = requests.get(
            f"{os.environ['SOURCE_ODATA_URL']}/{obj}Set/$count",
            headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {get_sap_token()}'}
        )
        counts[obj] = int(response.text)
    state['source_counts'] = counts
    return state
 
def cross_validate_counts(state: ReconciliationState) -> ReconciliationState:
    discrepancies = []
    for obj in state['migration_objects']:
        src=state['source_counts'].get(obj, 0)
        tgt = state['target_counts'].get(obj, 0)
        variance_pct = abs(src - tgt) / src * 100 if src > 0 else 0
        if variance_pct > 0.01:  # Flag >0.01% variance
            discrepancies.append({
                'object': obj, 'source': src, 'target': tgt,
                'variance_pct': round(variance_pct, 4),
                'delta': src - tgt
            })
    state['discrepancies'] = discrepancies
    return state
 
def generate_sign_off_report(state: ReconciliationState) -> ReconciliationState:
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP migration lead.
        Generate a post-migration sign-off report.
        Determine sign-off status: APPROVED (no significant gaps),
        CONDITIONAL (minor gaps with accepted risk), REJECTED (critical gaps).
        Include executive summary, object-level results, exception table,
        and recommendation. Format professionally for stakeholder review.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'''
Migration objects: {state['migration_objects']}
Source counts: {state['source_counts']}
Target counts: {state['target_counts']}
Discrepancies: {state['discrepancies']}
Investigated gaps: {state['investigated_gaps']}''')
    ]
    response = llm(messages).content
    # Extract sign-off status from response
    status = 'APPROVED' if 'APPROVED' in response else
             'CONDITIONAL' if 'CONDITIONAL' in response else 'REJECTED'
    state['sign_off_status'] = status
    state['sign_off_report'] = response
    return state

The sign-off report generated by Agent 5 is production-ready for stakeholder review. It includes the variance analysis, exception table, risk assessment, and go/no-go recommendation in a format that migration leads and project managers can use directly — reducing the post-migration documentation effort from days to minutes.

ORCHESTRATING ALL FIVE AGENTS 

Running the Full Migration Agent Pipeline

The five agents are designed to run sequentially with each agent's output feeding the next. This is the complete migration lifecycle orchestration:

# Full migration agent pipeline orchestration
def run_migration_agent_pipeline(sap_system, migration_type, log_source):
    print('Phase 1: Landscape Assessment...')
    assessment_result = assessment_agent.invoke({
        'sap_system': sap_system,
        'resource_group': 'default',
        'z_objects': [], 'interfaces': [], 'data_volumes': {},
        'risk_indicators': [], 'complexity_score': '', 'report': '', 'messages': []
    })
 
    print('Phase 2: Runbook Generation...')
    runbook_result = runbook_agent.invoke({
        'assessment_report': assessment_result['report'],
        'migration_type': migration_type,
        'migration_objects': [], 'object_runbooks': {},
        'rollback_plan': '', 'full_runbook': ''
    })
 
    print('Phase 3: Data Quality Validation...')
    # Run validation agent per object type
 
    print('Phase 4: Migration Execution Monitoring...')
    monitor_result = monitor_agent.invoke({
        'log_source': log_source,
        'last_processed_line': 0,
        'exceptions': [], 'critical_flags': [],
        'escalation_required': False,
        'migration_status': 'RUNNING', 'status_report': ''
    })
 
    print('Phase 5: Post-Migration Reconciliation...')
    recon_result = recon_agent.invoke({
        'migration_objects': runbook_result['migration_objects'],
        'source_counts': {}, 'target_counts': {},
        'discrepancies': [], 'investigated_gaps': [],
        'sign_off_status': '', 'sign_off_report': ''
    })
 
    return {
        'assessment':    assessment_result['report'],
        'runbook':       runbook_result['full_runbook'],
        'monitoring':    monitor_result['status_report'],
        'sign_off':      recon_result['sign_off_report'],
        'sign_off_status': recon_result['sign_off_status']
    }

DESIGN PRINCIPLES 

What Makes Migration Agents Production-Ready

 

Principle

Implementation Guidance

Human-in-the-loop on critical decisions

Agents flag — humans decide on CRITICAL exceptions, low-confidence resolutions, and REJECTED sign-offs. Never fully autonomous on irreversible migration actions.

Audit trail on every agent action

Every agent node logs its inputs, outputs, and AI reasoning to HANA Cloud. Migration auditors need to trace every automated decision post-go-live.

Read-only SAP connectivity

All five agents read from SAP systems — none write back. Writing is performed by the migration tool (DMLT, LTMC, etc.) after human review of agent outputs.

Graceful degradation

If an agent node fails, the pipeline logs the failure and produces a partial output rather than crashing. Incomplete outputs are more useful than no output.

Model selection per agent

GPT-4o for code analysis (Agent 1), validation logic (Agent 2), and log classification (Agent 4). Claude for documentation generation (Agents 3 and 5) where structured long-form output quality matters.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 5: Human-in-the-Loop SAP Agents — approval workflows, escalation patterns, and audit trail architecture for production agentic SAP systems
  • Part 6: Production Agentic SAP Systems — monitoring, observability, failure handling, and cost management for agents running at enterprise scale

The five agents in this article represent the most immediate ROI opportunity for agentic AI in SAP — the migration workstream is well-defined, the failure modes are known, and the documentation burden is enormous. If you implement any of these agents in your migration practice, share your results in the comments. The community variations are where the most valuable refinements emerge.

entry.title: Tolerance Group MM, not FI, in BPs master data
entry.content: Where in BP's master data I can maintain the field Tolerance Group. I know that this field is located in BP Role: FLVN00 - FI Vendor, Payment Transaction Tab. All the options available to choose are from transaction OBA3, table T043G and that tx and table are Finance configurations. MM configurations are corresponding to TX OMRX, table T169L. My question is where in BP master data can maintain this field from OMRX, table T169L?

entry.title: IPS (Identity Provisioning) to the IAS mirgation
entry.content:
How Do I know If I’m using the old IAS admin tool?

Just the fact that you are able to access to your IPS cockpit/ platform, means you have to do the migration.

Other way, is to verify your current menu in the IAS admin panel. ou will see a Menu of 3 items like the below:

 

Migration Wizard

A wizard guides you through the migration process.

First, you choose the target IAS tenant to which you want to migrate your Identity Provisioning tenant.

Then, you select the provisioning systems – source, target and proxy, regardless of their status (enabled or disabled).

 
The process moves the system configurations, such as properties, destinations, credentials and transformations, to the tenant in the new infrastructure. Provisioning job logs are not migrated. They are retained in your Neo tenant according to the period of time (7, 14 or 30 days) you have configured. The migration job log is retained for 30 days.

 

SAP Help about IPS migration to IAShttps://help.sap.com/docs/identity-provisioning/identity-provisioning/migrate-identity-provisioning-bundle-tenant
Great Bloghttps://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/go-for-your-quick-win-migrate-identity-provisioning-tenants-to-sap-cloud/ba-p/13536739
  

 

Migration Notice

You have received a message like below:

“Migration of your tenant to SAP Cloud Identity infrastructure is strongly encouraged. It brings significant advantages, such as new features and improved integration between SAP Cloud Identity Services.”

 

What does this means?: 

SAP is asking to migrate the IPS to the IAS infrastructure. The new SAP IAS panel holds inside an Identity Provisioning IPS. Doing this migration, IPS will not exist any more and everytihing will be managed from IAS.

The SAP IAS panel now integrates the SAP IPS panel into the same cockpit.

If your IAS looks like this, means you have NOT done the upgrade.

 

 Or, if your IPS look like this, also means the migration has not been done, and that it’s requiered.

Soon or later all the clients should have to do it. Don’t you worry, since is a easy process, but pay attention to the reccomendations below.

Once the migration is done the “Identity Provisoning” tab will appear.

 

Let’s get started!

Preparation

  • Ensure you have a bundle tenant running on SAP BTP. Standalone tenants cannot be migrated.
  • Ensure you have an admin user on the target IAS.
  • Ensure that no provisioning jobs are running before you start the migration. Stop manually triggered jobs and pause the scheduled ones.
  • If you use connectivity destination for any of your systems, do not modify them while the migration is running.

After migration, you will have access to your IPS tenant on Neo environment for 30 days. After that, the tenant is offboarded and cannot be restored. Although your Neo tenant will be available for 30 days, we recommend that you do not perform any operations on it, such as running jobs, adding provisioning systems and others.

Step by Step

 

  1. Click on Tenant Migration

 

The migration starting screen will appear:

 

Once you have selected the Source Systems and the Target ones, you can run the Wizard by clicking on the “Finish” button.

 

 

The Wizard is more or less fast.

 

Once the process is finished you will see a screenshot like below

 

After the Wizard has finished, all the source systems will still be enabled in IPS and disabled in IAS.

At the IAS panel > under Identity Provisioning  you will find the exact copy of target and source systems. The copied systemas are disabled and not scheduled.

 

The next step is to disable all the systems in IPS and enable in IAS. Run manually and check if everything goes ok.

 

Then you can consider the migration as finished!
